title: Apostrophes and plurals
description: Apostrophes are generally not used to form English plurals, but there are a few exceptions.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the option that correctly pluralizes the missing word in this sentence.** 

>All Carl ever wants to talk about is \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Typically, apostrophes are **not** used to form plurals.

Correct | Incorrect 
- | - 
${\text{three oranges}}$ | $\redD{\text{three orange's}}$
${\text{flat screen TVs}}$ | $\redD{\text{flat screen TV's}}$


You can use apostrophes to shorten dates, but not to pluralize them. 

Correct | Incorrect 
- | - 
${\text{1980s}}$ | $\redD{\text{1980's}}$
${\text{'80s}}$ | $\redD{\text{80's}}$

*However,* the one time you *do* use an apostrophe to form a plural is when you are pluralizing a single lowercase letter.

For example:

>*There are four p's in the word Mississipi.*
The correct plural form is **dinosaurs**.
